content.text: The right editor setup turns React development from a friction-heavy experience into a fluid one. VS Code has become the near-universal choice for React work because its TypeScript language server, JSX support, and extension ecosystem are purpose-built for the toolchain you will use daily: ESLint, Prettier, Vite, and the TypeScript compiler.

VS Code is free, open-source, and ships with a TypeScript language server that understands JSX and TSX natively. It can jump-to-definition through node_modules, infer prop types from component signatures, and refactor symbol names across the whole project — all without any extra configuration. Alternatives exist (WebStorm, Neovim with LSP, Zed), but VS Code's extension marketplace is where most React-specific tooling is published first.

attrs.alt: Table of essential VS Code extensions for React development with publisher IDs, descriptions, and categories

The ESLint extension runs the same rules that
runs — but as you type, not after you save. Red underlines appear under problematic code, and hovering shows the rule name so you can look it up. With
enabled on save, fixable problems (unused imports, missing semicolons, wrong quote style) are corrected automatically.
Prettier reformats your code to a consistent style every time you save. It handles line length, indentation, trailing commas, JSX attribute quoting, and more. The key setting is
file exists in the project root, the extension picks it up automatically.

VS Code bundles TypeScript language features, but by default it uses its own bundled version of TypeScript rather than the one installed in your project. This can cause subtle version mismatches. Point VS Code at your project's copy by adding
to your workspace settings. After that, VS Code's type checks, autocomplete, and error reporting match exactly what
This extension provides keyboard-triggered code expansions. Type a short prefix and press Tab to expand a full boilerplate snippet.
VS Code has two settings scopes: user (global) and workspace (per project). Workspace settings live in
and should be committed to the repository so every developer on the team gets the same editor behaviour automatically.
flag causes VS Code to ask (once per workspace) whether to use the project's TypeScript rather than its built-in copy. Combining it with
makes the switch automatic for all contributors.
You can tell VS Code which extensions the project recommends. When someone clones the repo and opens it, VS Code offers to install these automatically.
VS Code can attach its debugger to Vite's dev server so you can set breakpoints in your source files (not the compiled bundle). Create
Select "Launch Vite app in Chrome" and press F5.

Vite generates source maps in development mode by default (
for production to keep bundle size small). The launch config above maps the Vite virtual paths (

Separate from VS Code, install the React Developer Tools browser extension (available for Chrome and Firefox). It adds two panels to the browser DevTools:
— browse the full component tree, inspect props and state, trigger re-renders.
— record and replay renders to identify which components re-render on each interaction and why.
You cannot inspect React component state from the browser's built-in DevTools alone — the React DevTools extension is mandatory for this kind of introspection.

ESLint vs Prettier: Overlap and Resolution
ESLint has style rules (spacing, quotes) that overlap with Prettier. They can conflict. The standard fix is to install
and add it as the last item in your extends array — it disables all ESLint formatting rules so Prettier wins:
If you have both Prettier and the built-in JS formatter active, VS Code will ask which one to use every time. Fix by setting
explicitly per language in workspace settings.
ESLint extension using the wrong config version.
. The VS Code ESLint extension ≥ 3.0 supports both. If errors appear in the Output > ESLint panel, it is usually a config format mismatch.

Check that the file is saved as UTF-8 (not a read-only file), that Prettier's output channel in VS Code shows no parse errors, and that
is not overridden by a user-level setting.
The ES7+ snippets extension activates for

Paid IDE, built-in refactoring, no config needed
High setup cost; same TypeScript LSP under the hood
Native Rust editor, fast; smaller extension library
Install: ESLint, Prettier, ES7+ Snippets, Error Lens, Path IntelliSense.

Integrating Prettier and ESLint into the Save Cycle
Getting format-on-save and lint-on-save to cooperate reliably requires understanding the order VS Code applies them. When you save a file:
— it applies auto-fixable lint rules (removes unused imports, fixes exhaustive-deps where possible).
— it reformats the result to canonical style.

Because Prettier runs after ESLint it will reformat any code ESLint touched, so there is no conflict. If ESLint reports style errors that Prettier should own (semicolons, quote style), fix that by adding
as shown in the previous section.
Using Multi-Cursor and IntelliSense Effectively with React
VS Code's multi-cursor editing is particularly powerful for React component work. Common patterns:
Rename a prop everywhere it is used
— press F2 on the prop name in the interface definition. VS Code renames every usage across all files that import the component.
Find all places a component is used
— Shift+F12 on the component name shows every import and JSX usage site across the project.
, click the lightbulb (or Ctrl+. / Cmd+.) and VS Code offers "Add type annotation" in-place.
— select JSX in your file, press Ctrl+. / Cmd+., and choose "Extract to function component" to automatically pull it into a named function.
VS Code ships Emmet support but does not enable it for JSX files by default. Add this to your workspace settings to get Emmet abbreviation expansion inside
and pressing Tab expands it to a complete JSX tree of a
Workspace-Level TypeScript Errors vs Problems Panel
VS Code's TypeScript language server only type-checks
by default. Files not open in any tab may have type errors that the Problems panel does not show. Two ways to see all errors:
in the terminal — checks the entire project as configured in
Enable workspace-level diagnostics: open the command palette, run "TypeScript: Enable project-wide IntelliSense" — VS Code then checks all included files continuously.
step in most Vite + TypeScript projects runs
, meaning a type error will break the build even if it wasn't visible in the editor. Running
locally before pushing confirms there are no surprises on CI.
VS Code has built-in Git support accessible via the Source Control panel (Ctrl+Shift+G / Cmd+Shift+G). For React projects this means:
Modified lines are highlighted in the editor gutter (green for added, blue for modified, red triangle for deleted).
Clicking a gutter marker opens an inline diff so you can review exactly what changed without leaving the file.
The GitLens extension adds inline blame annotations — hover over any line to see who last changed it and the commit message.
Stage individual hunks (not whole files) from the diff view — useful when you want to commit part of a file's changes and leave the rest unstaged.
React projects accumulate many co-located files per component:
. The VS Code file nesting feature groups related files under their parent in the Explorer, keeping the tree readable:
and can be expanded by clicking the arrow. The project root also becomes cleaner because all the config files nest under
If an extension behaves unexpectedly, VS Code's built-in bisect tool isolates the culprit:
Open the command palette and run "Extension Bisect: Start".
VS Code disables half your extensions and asks if the problem is still present.
Answer yes/no repeatedly until VS Code identifies the single conflicting extension.
You can also start VS Code with all extensions disabled (
) and re-enable them one at a time to confirm a conflict.
setting can trigger format-on-save automatically after a delay. However, for React development with Vite's HMR, explicit save (
) is generally preferable to afterDelay because:
Vite's HMR fires on every save — auto-saving mid-edit may trigger HMR before the component is valid JSX, causing a visible error flash in the browser.
ESLint and TypeScript recheck on every save; frequent auto-saves can slow the CPU noticeably on large projects.
— this saves when you switch away from the file rather than after a timer.

Beyond the ES7+ Snippets extension, you can define project-specific snippets in
and commit them to the repository. This is useful for repetitive patterns unique to your codebase — a standard page layout, a data-fetching hook, a form field pattern.

If your project uses Storybook, the
A sidebar panel listing all stories without opening the browser.
CodeLens actions above each story definition to "Open in Storybook" with a single click.
(vitest.explorer) integrates the test runner directly into the editor:
A Test Explorer panel shows all test files and individual test cases.
Green/red indicators appear inline next to each test function as tests run in watch mode.
You can run or debug a single test file or single
block from the CodeLens action that appears above each test.
VS Code is the standard React editor because its built-in TypeScript server understands JSX and TSX without configuration.
The core extensions are ESLint (inline linting + auto-fix) and Prettier (format on save); together they eliminate most style and correctness issues before code review.

Install the React Developer Tools browser extension for component tree inspection and performance profiling.
